title: Ethiopian Doro Wat (Spiced Chicken Stew)

subs.name: Bone-in mutton pieces for a richer, meatier version; increase braising time to 50 minutes
Skip the eggs for a straightforward chicken stew; or use paneer cubes scored and added at the same step

ingredients.name: red onion, very finely minced
visual_checks: The onion cooks dry without oil first — this slow caramelisation forms the flavour base
ingredients.notes: The onion cooks dry without oil first — this slow caramelisation forms the flavour base

ingredients.name: red chilli powder (Kashmiri or cayenne for berbere heat)
visual_checks: Berbere spice blend is traditional; approximate it with extra chilli + the spices listed below
ingredients.notes: Berbere spice blend is traditional; approximate it with extra chilli + the spices listed below

subs.name: Use Kashmiri chilli powder for a milder, deeply red version with less heat but richer colour

ingredients.name: ghee or spiced niter kibbeh (clarified butter)
visual_checks: Niter kibbeh is Ethiopian spiced butter; plain ghee is the best Indian substitute
ingredients.notes: Niter kibbeh is Ethiopian spiced butter; plain ghee is the best Indian substitute

subs.name: Butter or oil; ghee is closest to niter kibbeh (Ethiopian spiced butter) that is traditional

steps.text: Score the hard-boiled eggs all over with a fork or knife tip — this allows the sauce to penetrate during cooking. Prick the chicken pieces deeply with a fork as well. Toss chicken with lemon juice and a pinch of salt and let it rest while you start the onion.

In a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat with no oil, add the very finely minced red onion. Cook dry, stirring frequently, for 20 minutes until the onion is completely dry, deep caramel-brown and reduced to less than a third of its original volume — this is the heart of doro wat. Do not rush this step.

Add the ghee to the caramelised onion and stir to combine. Add garlic and ginger; stir for 2 minutes until fragrant. Add red chilli powder, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves and black pepper; stir for 1 minute on medium-low flame until the spices are absorbed into the oil and the colour deepens to a brick red.

Add the chicken pieces and stir to coat in the thick spiced onion paste. Pour in 300 ml water, bring to a boil, then reduce the flame to low, cover and simmer for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the chicken is cooked through and the sauce has darkened and thickened.

Nestle the scored hard-boiled eggs gently into the sauce. Cover and cook on low for 10 more minutes — the eggs will absorb the sauce colour and flavour through the scored surface. Taste for salt and adjust.

The finished doro wat should have a thick, glossy dark-red sauce that coats the chicken with rich, layered spice. Serve over injera (Ethiopian flatbread), rice or any flatbread — the sauce is meant to be scooped up generously.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → Bone-in mutton pieces for a richer, meatier version; increase braising time to 50 minutes; ghee → Butter or oil; ghee is closest to niter kibbeh (Ethiopian spiced butter) that is traditional; egg → Skip the eggs for a straightforward chicken stew; or use paneer cubes scored and added at the same step; red-chilli-powder → Use Kashmiri chilli powder for a milder, deeply red version with less heat but richer colour
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook in a kadai or heavy handi. Berbere spice blend can be assembled from the Indian pantry: red chilli powder, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, coriander seeds (ground) and black pepper in the proportions listed. Niter kibbeh (Ethiopian spiced butter) is best replaced with plain ghee — the flavour is very close. The dry-caramelising of onions without oil is the crucial step; do not skip it.
